[180:09]whether it's the Gaza genocide,

[180:10]they're dragging us into this war.

[180:12]People like Ben Shapiro seem to care more about Israel.

[180:15]The endless,

[180:17]admonitions about antisemitism and the visibility of the Holocaust everywhere, the over-representation

[180:24]of Jews in powerful positions. Like, people are making these observations, they're noticing,

[180:29]something's not quite right. What needs to be done now is to truly define the issue

[180:35]and spell out very clearly and precisely what is happening, the true nature and the extent and the

[180:45]how to think about it, kind of the bigger context of it,

[180:50]how to interpret it through a religious, political,

[180:52]or ethnic lens, and then prescribe what to do about it.

[180:57]And even talking about a prescription,

[181:00]it almost sounds like I'm insinuating something

[181:03]which I'm not.

[181:03]That's how scared people are of these ideas.

[181:08]People are just asking questions.

[181:10]People are just noticing.

[181:12]But you start to talk about moving the ball down the field, which is to say, okay people. I think it's safe to say have noticed

[181:20]People it's safe to say have asked the questions and whether or not they've received direct answers. They're coming to conclusions

[181:28]But now it is time. I think to shed some light on it

[181:32]Let's get the subject out of the dark out of the realm of

[181:36]implication and insinuation and just asking questions. It's time to put it in the broad light

[181:42]of day with no shadows and no, um, no ambiguity and say precisely what is going on specifically

[181:55]And then having understood the problem, start to figure out how we're going to group people

[182:00]together in a political coalition to do something about it.

[182:04]And that is always what makes people worried.

[182:07]People love to complain.

[182:09]And like I said, notice and make insinuations, but you start to say, well, what are we going

[182:14]to do?

[182:14]Which is of course the operative question.

[182:19]If our country is unrecognizable, if there is this hostile alien overclass that is inflicting

[182:26]really negative governance on the country, then how do we stop that from happening?

[182:33]How do we reverse that?

[182:34]And that's the tricky part.

[182:36]And that's where you get a lot of bad actors that come in and they start talking about

[182:49]because I think that's always there.

[182:51]Now that people are noticing the next stage is they're going to get some guy who hates

[182:55]Jews to go and do a mass shooting and then they're going to say, oh, now we need to have

[182:59]mass censorship.

[183:11]We are peaceful, we are Christian, we desire to make reasonable changes using our political

[183:18]system, using our constitutional rights, using our democratic mode of governance.

[183:24]And all we are seeking to do is to protect the integrity of the government.

[183:44]You want to harm and injure and ultimately murder people for who they are and that is absolutely not the case

[183:52]That has never been the case. My religion is Jesus Christ. I'm a Catholic. We love all people

[184:00]And inspired by that, motivated by that,

[184:03]by our theology, by our spirituality and religion,

[184:06]we want a system that protects the dignity and the rights

[184:10]and the life of all people, all people living in our country

[184:14]and even for that matter in other countries too.

[184:17]I believe that we have an obligation

[184:19]as a country on the world stage

[184:21]to even not treat people in an inhumane way,

[184:24]for example, in Gaza for that matter or elsewhere.

[184:32]And we have to start thinking about the future,

[184:38]where they're gonna go next

[184:49]and that is not what we're about at all.

[184:56]we're not racist, we're not anti-Semitic,

[184:58]whatever the fuck that means.

[184:59]I don't even know what those words mean.

[185:07]things like registering foreign agents.

[185:12]We don't want foreign influence over our government.

[185:32]for all.

[185:34]It's not a Jewish country.

[185:45]This is a nice place, and who we are is what made it this way.

[185:49]Who the founders were, the founding stock, which I'm not even a part of.

[191:08]And our first story we're gonna talk all about the birthright citizenship executive order

[191:12]Which was adjudicated in the Supreme Court today. That's the big news

[191:17]And the development, although a lot of people believe that the Supreme Court ruled on the

[191:22]birthright citizenship policy itself, what the decision is really about is whether the

[191:29]injunction against that policy will be able to stand.

[191:36]And so a quick refresher for those that haven't been following the story or the show, back

[191:42]in January, President Trump signed an executive order that would revoke birthright citizenship.

[191:49]Birthright citizenship comes from a constitutional amendment after the Civil War.

[191:54]And after the Civil War, there was a question about whether the slaves, who were technically

[192:00]property and in a distinct legal category, once freed, would they become citizens and

[192:07]enjoy legal and social equality with the white people?

[192:12]And so birthright citizenship is in a provision in the three amendments after the end of the

[192:23]on the soil, you are a citizen.

[192:27]Now understand, this is an historical peculiarity.

[192:32]This is not from the founding documents.

[192:35]This is not from the Constitution as it was framed

[192:40]and drafted in the late 18th century.

[192:45]This comes from the Civil War

[192:46]and it was meant to remedy a very specific situation.

[192:50]This was not the design of the founding fathers

[192:52]This was not even the design of a particular administration to say that anyone that is born on the soil is going to be an American

[193:02]This was a remedy for a specific

[193:05]Problem in America, which is slavery and its termination. What do we do with all these people?

[193:12]Well, they're gonna have citizenship

[193:14]What it has become since then, and this was adjudicated 30 years after that, is a license

[193:21]for anyone in the world to come to America, have their child here, and now they have established

[193:29]a bridge that they can live indefinitely inside of our country and enjoy all the benefits

[193:35]of the American taxpayer.

[193:37]And this is a framework.

[193:42]over 150 years.

[193:44]At the time of the civil war, you have no social security, you have no Medicare, you

[193:49]have no Department of Education, you have no welfare, you have no income tax, you don't

[193:54]even have the Federal Reserve, you don't have hordes of immigrants coming in.

[194:00]You ultimately did.

[194:01]shortly before and then shortly after the civil war.

[194:04]But they were coming in from Europe.

[194:06]Now fast forward 150 years later

[194:09]and what this policy means

[194:10]rather than the freed slaves are gonna have citizenship

[194:13]and it's not a legal problem.

[194:15]150 years later, this is a license for people

[194:18]from China or Russia to do birth tourism

[194:21]where an immigrant comes in.

[194:23]In order to have their child, they'll come in as a tourist, they'll come in on some other

[194:28]kind of visa, and it might not even be longer than three months. But they come in with an

[194:34]expectant mother, they have their child, the child's a citizen. And now that child is entitled to

[194:40]all the benefits that that is, that citizenship confers.

[194:44]They get to vote in our elections.

[194:46]They get the benefits of social security.

[194:49]They get the benefits of public schooling.

[194:51]They get the benefits of being able to stay and reside in America, to go to our universities,

[194:56]to get work, to do any number of things.

[194:59]And it's also true of illegal immigrants.

[195:02]This is a big part of the illegal immigrant problem.

[195:11]And the reason being is because let's say we had 10 million illegal immigrants and they

[195:16]were working on farms and in construction and in hospitality.

[195:21]And they're being employed under the table.

[195:24]They're cheap labor.

[195:26]their unskilled labor, and this allows agriculture and construction and hotels to have lower prices.

[195:34]And these jobs, which are really unattractive, well, you know, they're gonna be done by this

[195:39]imported slave class, this like indentured servant class.

[195:43]And there's a trade-off.

[195:44]These illegals that are here, they're sending remittances back home.

[195:47]The money they're earning, they're sending back to their family.

[195:54]or they'll come here and they'll go back and forth

[195:57]between the United States and Mexico.

[195:59]And theoretically, at some date in the future,

[196:03]theoretically, we could always get rid of them.

[196:06]And we could treat them as a temporary imported working class.

[196:13]We could treat them as foreign workers.

[196:16]And they would be altogether a distinct category

[196:19]of people living in our country.

[196:21]And that would be effectively what it is.

[196:24]And, theoretically, that's how we could treat it.

[196:27]We're gonna bring in some illegal immigrants, they'll do construction, they'll do agriculture,

[196:32]agriculture.

[196:34]And when we don't have use for them, when we don't have use for their labor or they

[196:38]become a problem in one way or another, then, theoretically, we could always deport them.

[196:47]But I'm saying this speaks to why birthright citizenship is really the biggest problem

[196:53]with illegal immigration.

[196:54]Because although theoretically, if people came and worked and they didn't have citizenship,

[197:00]we could always have the discretion to remove them once they have children, because of course

[197:10]Because they're people and not workers,

[197:13]what they're gonna do is they're gonna come here

[197:14]and they're gonna find a wife.

[197:16]Or they're gonna come here with their wife.

[197:20]and they're gonna have more kids.

[197:25]is gonna become a citizen.

[197:27]That's why they call them anchor babies

[197:28]because it's anchors away.

[197:30]Whereas before we can bring them in and ship them out at will and I say theoretically because of course

[197:36]These people are never deported. They're probably gonna be here forever no matter what but you would have the discretion to do it

[197:43]Because they have birthright citizenship now. They're not going anywhere and with the citizenship

[197:55]The cousins can come the uncles everybody can come over

[198:00]And for many decades, this chain migration has been one of the major sources.

[198:06]The family-based migration has been a major source of even our legal immigration.

[198:18]kids, and this becomes a family tree.

[198:21]and they're multiplying, and then we don't know what's what.

[198:31]They've got to claim to the land.

[198:33]They've got to claim to be a citizen of our country.

[198:36]And now they start saying we're American.

[198:44]the table on farms. They don't speak English. They're not part of any semblance of community.

[198:56]travel back and forth between the US and Mexico. They're sending money out of our country.

[199:19]fluke because they got dropped on the land. Now they're just as American as the descendants

[199:23]of George Washington. I don't think so.

[199:29]And it's one of the, I don't know if it's the central,

[199:32]but it's one of the central issues with illegal immigration.

[199:36]So Trump passed an executive order in January,

[199:39]and he said that we're no longer granting citizenship

[199:42]to people that are born on the land.

[199:44]To be a naturalized citizen,

[199:46]you need parents that are Americans.

[199:49]You need parents that have citizenship

[199:51]in order for the citizenship to be passed down.

[199:54]You can't just be born on the land,

[199:55]even if both your parents are foreign nationals.

[199:58]Even if your parents are illegal immigrants, you need to have parents that are citizens

[200:02]also.

[200:03]And by the way, this is how it is in the rest of the world.

[200:07]If you look at a map of which countries have citizenship from being born on the land, there

[200:12]are almost no countries in Europe, Asia, in Africa that have it this way.

[200:18]It's only in the United States and in Latin America that you have birthright citizenship.

[200:27]For obvious reasons, which is shocking, because this is an empire, you know, the United States is an empire.

[200:33]We should have the most sophisticated labor and immigration policies. These things are deeply related.

[200:39]But we are being screwed over, basically, by this technicality from the 19th century.

[200:47]And anyway.

[200:48]So Trump passes this executive order in January and immediately automatically a federal judge,

[200:54]which is at the lowest level of the federal judiciary in a district court, files a nationwide

[201:01]injunction against the policy.

[201:04]Now understand constitutionally, the way our system works, if you don't know, is that

[201:15]That's the, that's the job of the executive branch. The executive means to execute. We

[201:21]execute the laws that are passed by the Congress. And so the president's job, being the head

[201:28]of the executive branch,

[201:31]But his job is in some ways to interpret the law.

[201:34]All laws have to be interpreted in order to be enforced.

[201:38]And so with the reading of our immigration laws and our constitution, the president signs

[201:42]an executive order for the enforcement of the law and says, we're not granting citizenship.

[201:47]Our federal system is not giving citizenship anymore.

[201:51]to illegal immigrants. The slaves got their citizenship and their children did too. And

[201:55]that's it. No more illegal immigrants and their children are getting citizenship on this technicality.

[202:02]A district judge files a nationwide injunction of which there are many districts and says, nope,

[202:09]you can't do that.

[202:11]And now it's gonna take a year and a half, a full 18 months, maybe, that's at the earliest.

[202:18]For this decision, this injunction, now to make its way up all the way through the federal

[202:24]court system to the Supreme Court, for the Supreme Court to make a decision on whether

[202:29]the executive order is constitutional or not, they shut it down, they vetoed it.

[202:40]The American president is elected by the electoral college representatives from all 50 states.

[202:46]And now because of our voting system, all of the people take part in this election.

[202:51]The highest member of the executive branch is vetoed by one of the lowest members of

[202:57]the judicial branch.

[202:58]How does that make any sense?

[203:01]And this has been a problem under Democrat and Republican presidents.

[203:09]or it's a law review, it says that virtually every single executive order that has been

[203:15]passed in contemporary history has been automatically shut down with a nationwide injunction by

[203:23]a federal judge.

[203:24]So what power does the president have?

[203:27]Think about it.

[203:28]The president can't pass executive orders.

[203:30]They will be shut down by the lowest level of the judicial branch.

[203:35]The president cannot appoint certain people to run the government because it has to go

[203:40]through the Senate for confirmation and receive a super majority because of their filibuster

[203:45]rule.

[203:46]So the president needs 60 votes to hire people, needs the ascent of all the district judges

[203:51]to pass any executive order.

[203:54]The president can't fire people.

[203:56]President can only fire 5,000 people if he fires anybody else.

[204:04]So, what power exactly does the president have?

[204:08]Can't pass terrorists shut down by the courts.

[204:11]Can't go to war with Iran challenged in the Congress.

[204:15]And I wasn't in favor of that, but I think that's the president's discretion, actually.

[204:20]Can't hire and fire people, gotta go through Congress, gotta go through the courts.

[204:25]Can't pass an executive order on citizenship or apparently anything.

[204:29]Gotta go through every single federal judge.

[204:35]So it is this injunction, which was adjudicated in the Supreme Court today, and the Supreme

[204:42]Court in a 6-3 decision said that district judges, federal judges, can no longer issue

[204:48]nationwide injunctions against executive orders like this.

[204:52]That is the decision that was made.

[204:55]And what happens now is that the injunction against the executive order, and injunction

[205:00]means that they can't, the executive order won't go into effect.

[205:05]The Supreme Court ruling says that the injunction is nullified.

[205:09]And if the injunction is nullified, then that means the executive order goes into effect.

[205:15]In 30 days from this decision, the injunction is nullified.

[205:21]Now, there are 32 states that have a challenge against the executive order.

[205:28]In 28 states, there is not a challenge.

[205:31]So it seems that in 30 days, this birthright citizenship executive order will go into effect

[205:36]for 28 states, meaning that there will be a patchwork system.

[205:41]In 28 states, you do not have birthright citizenship.

[205:50]You don't have citizenship.

[205:52]If you're born in one of the 32 states that does have it, I guess you will get your citizenship.

[205:58]There's also some loopholes that's part of this ruling.

[206:01]The ruling says that the problem is that the injunction process has been abused.

[206:08]Any federal judge.

[206:10]can have an injunction against any decision from the president.

[206:15]And what this gives way to is that you'll have a plaintiff

[206:19]will shop around for a favorable jurisdiction.

[206:22]They'll go to the most liberal federal judge

[206:25]and they'll file their challenge against the executive order there.

[206:28]They'll go to a liberal judge that they know is politically biased

[206:32]and they can count on that judge to deliver an injunction for anything.

[206:37]And the Supreme Court said obviously this isn't gonna work, you're abusing the system.

[206:42]What the Supreme Court says you can do instead is file a class action lawsuit.

[206:49]And what a class action does is it can protect an entire class of people that are affected

[206:54]by a decision.

[206:55]So in this case, it would be those people who would have birthright citizenship.

[207:05]it can affect the entire class across the whole nation.

[207:08]But there's a higher bar for a class action suit

[207:11]than there is for a nationwide injunction.

[207:15]So you can have the same effect of a nationwide injunction,

[207:18]but it's more difficult to get,

[207:21]and you have to meet a certain standard.

[207:24]It's a little bit of a higher bar.

[207:27]And now what remains to be seen is how that aspect of it is interpreted.

[207:31]That is one out of three loopholes, there's a couple of others that can be used to get

[207:36]around this ruling.

[207:38]And this is the story.

[207:39]This is from the New York Times.

[207:41]It says, quote, the Supreme Court ruled on Friday in a decision that limited the ability

[207:48]of lower court judges to block executive branch policies nationwide, opening the door for

[207:53]a majority of states to at least temporarily enforce President Trump's executive order,

[207:57]ending birthright citizenship.

[208:00]With their decision, the justices appeared to upend the ability of single federal judges

[208:07]The powerful tool known as a nationwide injunction had been used frequently in recent years to

[208:12]block policies put in place by Democrat and Republican administrations and gave rise to

[208:18]charges of judge shopping.

[208:21]The decision cleared the way for the executive order on birthright citizenship to go into

[208:25]effect in the 28 States that have not challenged it.

[208:28]which would create a patchwork system in which the rules for citizenship are

[208:32]different in different parts of the country. Still the Supreme Court ruling

[208:36]will not take effect for 30 days and the justices laid out a potential path for

[208:41]challengers saying that the district court judges could still consider whether

[208:45]to take up class action lawsuits seeking to bar enforcement of the executive

[208:55]Indeed, groups challenging the executive order immediately seized on that opening with class

[209:00]action filings in Maryland and New Hampshire.

[209:03]Other challengers are likely to do the same thing in the coming days.

[209:08]Justice Amy Coney Barrett cited examples of several justices criticizing nationwide injunctions

[209:14]and quoted an influential law review article that argued that by the end of the Biden administration,

[209:20]nearly every major presidential act had been immediately blocked by a federal trial judge.

[209:26]So this is a no-brainer. And I think that there's two ways to look at this.

[209:32]And it depends on how it goes.

[209:35]Now, of course, this is a huge problem.

[209:38]If every major executive order in both administrations is getting blocked, there's a problem here.

[209:45]This is a constitutional problem.

[209:46]This is a legal problem.

[209:47]And it has nothing to do with what's actually being done.

[209:50]It has to do with how it's being done.

[209:54]The president seemingly, and again, no matter whether it's Democrat or Republican, cannot

[210:03]Because the moment they do, the most right wing or the most left wing federal judge at

[210:08]the lowest level is gonna send up an injunction and block it for a year and a half.

[210:13]And within a year or two years, you're gonna have another election.

[210:18]At that point, it's, it is a form of veto power.

[210:23]This is a form of nullification and it is a constitutional problem.

[210:32]President Trump. He has all these executive orders. They're all getting blocked by almost

[210:38]every single one. Even the more reasonable ones are being challenged and given injunctions.

[210:44]So one way to look at this, depending on how this goes is this is going to free up the

[210:49]administration and now they're going to pass all kinds of executive orders and there's

[210:53]going to be no problems. And he's going to be able to rule and it's going to be great.

[210:59]If you actually read the ruling, which I think is 26 pages,

[211:03]and if you read the details,

[211:06]clearly it hasn't gotten rid of the power

[211:08]of federal judges to shut down an executive order.

[211:12]It just makes it harder for them to do that.

[211:16]So does this fundamentally change our system?

[211:20]I think the answer is no.

[211:22]They're not going to be able to use an injunction,

[211:25]but they are going to be able to use

[211:26]a class action lawsuit.

[211:27]It's just one example.

[211:29]And there's a higher bar, so it might take longer and more resources and it'll be narrower

[211:34]and less frequent, but they can still do it.

[211:39]And Justice Alito has said that, he said that depending on how this goes, we could be looking

[211:45]back at this as the realm of academic scholars.

[211:50]10, 20 years down the line, no one's gonna say this was a landmark decision that reversed

[211:54]the power of the federal judiciary.

[211:57]They're gonna say this is a technical problem and a technical solution.

[212:02]And this is the purview of legal scholars.

[212:04]It's not going to be politically as significant.

[212:09]And so I guess we'll see.

[212:17]And we'll have to see within these 30 days whether there'll be a class action suit and

[213:32]it's just such a joke.

[213:34]And think about immigration in this way.

[213:39]for our economy.

[213:40]I hear this all the time.

[213:41]They say, we need the labor for our economy.

[213:47]someone needs to build the houses.

[213:53]or clean the bedrooms at the hotels.

[213:58]Well, do you know there's such a thing as a foreign working population?

[214:04]Like we could create that, we could create a legal distinction in our country.

[214:08]Like there are entire countries that are built on this, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates.

[214:14]These countries are built on foreign labor.

[214:17]They have small populations.

[214:19]They don't really have a strong sense of national identity.

[214:23]And they bring in workers from Pakistan.

[214:28]They bring these people in, and it's a form of economic opportunity. It's a deal.

[214:33]These people from poor countries come into rich countries for a job.

[214:38]They agree to it. It's voluntary. It's contractual, and it's hard labor.

[214:43]You know, they accuse them of slave labor. It's not easy,

[214:45]but they get paid money. They get an opportunity. They live in safety.

[214:49]It's a little better than where they're from.

[214:51]And then when the job is over, they go home.

[214:55]Then, when the contract is up, they go back to where they came from.

[215:10]Give them visas, but make the visas expire.

[215:16]People say there's benefits to immigration, and I would say that the economic benefits

[215:22]For reasons we could get into

[215:24]But if that's the argument, you know, we need to satisfy or fill a certain quota of these types of jobs

[215:31]Then give them a certain kind of visa and say they could stay here on an annual basis and we renew it every year

[216:21]the program, I think far fewer people would have a problem with it. If that's desirable,

[216:27]if that's what you want to do, I don't think anybody would have an issue.

[216:41]in here on a visa and they overstayed the visa.

[216:46]They come in here and they blend in with the population.

[216:50]They come in here. There's no rules. There's no enforcement.

[217:01]Even if we could detain them, the backlog is a mile long.

[217:11]and then it takes resources to send them back.

[217:18]We couldn't even enforce the rules if we wanted to, even if we tried.

[217:28]resources. And it was designed that way. They crashed the system. They overwhelmed the system
